integrating-smart-home-technology-in-you.title: Integrating Smart Home Technology in Your Building Project
integrating-smart-home-technology-in-you.description: Imagine stepping into a home where the lights adjust to your mood, the temperature is always perfect and your favorite music starts playing as soon as you walk in. This is not a scene from a sci-fi movie - it's the reality of building with smart home technology.

blocks.text: Integrating Smart Home Technology in Your Building Project

Imagine stepping into a home where the lights adjust to your mood, the temperature is always perfect and your favorite music starts playing as soon as you walk in. This is not a scene from a sci-fi movie - it's the reality of building with smart home technology. This guide will help you understand how to integrate smart home tech into your building project.
1. Evaluate the Benefits of Smart Home Technology
So, why take the plunge and start building with smart home technology? Let's break it down:

Convenience: How cool would it be if you could control your home's lighting, heating, and even security systems with just a few swipes on your phone? This is the kind of convenience smart home technology offers. You can even set routines for your devices to follow, like having your coffee ready when you wake up. Now, who wouldn't want that?

Energy Efficiency: Building with smart home technology isn't just about the cool factor - it also makes sense for the planet and your pocket. Smart thermostats and lighting systems can adapt to your habits, ultimately conserving energy and reducing your bills.

Safety and Security: How many times have you left your home and then wondered if you locked the front door? With smart locks, you'll never have to worry about that again. And that's just the beginning - there are smart smoke detectors, security cameras, and more.
Increased Home Value: If you're building a home with an eye on future resale value, integrating smart home technology can make your property more attractive to tech-savvy buyers.

Remember, building with smart home technology is not just about adding gadgets for the sake of it. It's about enhancing the way you live in your home. The benefits are clear, so let's move on to picking the right devices. Shall we?
2. Select Appropriate Smart Home Devices

You've got the smart home bug. You're ready to dive in. But with the myriad of devices out there, where should you start? Here's a little guide to help you navigate the landscape of smart home technology:

Smart Speakers and Displays: Think of these like the command center of your smart home. Devices like the Amazon Echo or Google Home allow you to control all your smart devices through voice commands. Plus, they can answer your questions, play music, and more.

Smart Lighting: This is a great starting point for building with smart home technology. Brands like Philips Hue offer smart bulbs that you can control remotely, set up routines for, and even change the color of. It's a bright idea, wouldn't you agree?

Smart Thermostats: Devices like the Nest Thermostat can learn your routines and adjust the temperature accordingly. They can even detect when you're not home and switch to an energy-saving mode. Now that's smart!
Smart Security: This could include anything from smart locks and doorbells like those from Ring, to security cameras and even smart smoke detectors. Peace of mind has never been so high-tech.

Remember, the best smart home devices for you depend on your needs and lifestyle. So, take a moment to consider what areas of your home life could use a dash of smartness. And once you have that figured out, it's time to plan for integration in your building design. Are you excited yet? I know I am!
3. Plan for Integration in Building Design

Alright, you've selected your smart home devices. Now it's time to roll up your sleeves and get down to the nitty-gritty: integrating them into your building design. This is where building with smart home technology gets really interesting!
Understanding the Infrastructure

First things first, you need to understand the infrastructure requirements of your selected devices. This could be as simple as ensuring you have enough power outlets in the right places. Or, it could involve more complex considerations, like whether your WiFi network can handle all those devices.

Power Requirements: Make sure your building design provides ample and conveniently located power outlets for your smart home devices. Trust me, the last thing you want is a spaghetti junction of extension cords in your beautifully designed home.

WiFi Coverage: Smart home devices rely heavily on WiFi. So, it's crucial to ensure your home design allows for excellent WiFi coverage. Consider the placement of your router and whether you might need WiFi extenders.
Planning for Future Upgrades
Smart home technology is constantly evolving, and you don't want to be left in the dust. When building with smart home technology, always consider the future.
Scalability: Make sure your setup is scalable. This could mean choosing a smart home system that allows for easy addition of new devices.
Upgradability: Plan for the possibility of upgrading devices. For instance, if you're installing smart light switches, make sure the wiring can accommodate possible future upgrades.

Remember, planning ahead can save you a lot of headaches down the line. And with all the planning done, it's time to move onto the next exciting phase: implementation! Ready to bring your smart home dreams to life? Let's do this!
4. Implement Smart Home Technology in Construction
Fantastic! With the planning phase completed, it's time to get your hands dirty. Let's dive right into the implementation of your smart home technology.
Start with the Basics
Before we get too ahead of ourselves, let's start with the basics. This includes the installation of the necessary infrastructure to support your smart devices.

Wiring: Depending on your smart home devices, you may need to install special wiring. This could be Ethernet cables for a more stable internet connection, or specific wiring for smart switches or thermostats.
Router Placement: Remember when we talked about WiFi coverage? Now it's time to put that plan into action. Select a central location for your router to ensure optimal coverage throughout your home.
Bringing Your Smart Home to Life
Once the basics are in place, it's time to bring your smart home to life. This is the moment we've been building towards - literally!
Installation of Devices: Begin installing your smart devices. This could be anything from smart light bulbs and smart switches to smart refrigerators and smart thermostats. The sky's the limit!
Setup and Testing: After installing your devices, it's time to set them up and test them. Make sure they're all working as expected and communicating with each other effectively.

And there you have it! You've successfully navigated the process of building with smart home technology. Give yourself a pat on the back, and enjoy your new smart home. Now, wasn't that worth all the hard work?

modern-casita-and-guest-house-design-tre.title: Modern Casita and Guest House Design Trends in Arizona for 2026
modern-casita-and-guest-house-design-tre.description: Explore the latest casita and guest house design trends for 2026, including layout ideas, outdoor integration, energy efficient construction, and zoning guidance for Scottsdale, Phoenix, and surrounding Arizona communities.

blocks.text: Modern Casita and Guest House Design Trends in Arizona for 2026

Casitas and guest houses continue to rise in popularity across Scottsdale, Phoenix, Mesa, and surrounding parts of Arizona. These detached living spaces are no longer limited to simple guest quarters. Homeowners are transforming them into flexible, high value additions that serve as workspaces, rental units, private suites, and long term investment assets. As more cities update zoning rules and encourage thoughtful infill development, 2026 is shaping up to be a major year for casita design.
This guide explores the most important trends, layout ideas, and zoning considerations Arizona homeowners should understand before beginning a casita or guest house project.
Why Arizona Homeowners Are Building More Casitas
Growing Demand for Flexible Space

Casitas offer privacy and separation while staying connected to the main home. Buyers across the Phoenix metro increasingly want dedicated spaces for work, visiting family, fitness, or part time rental income.
Rising Home Values
A well designed casita can boost resale value, especially in Scottsdale and Cave Creek where larger lots and luxury features attract buyers looking for multi functional properties.
Changing Lifestyle Needs
Remote work, frequent guests, and the desire for private retreats have all contributed to the growing interest in detached living spaces.
Design Trends for 2026
Private Entry Suites
Casitas with their own entrances and small courtyards are a top trend. These layouts offer independence for guests, renters, or young adults while keeping them close to the main home.
Compact Luxury
Even smaller casitas are incorporating premium finishes. This includes quartz countertops, custom cabinetry, high quality flooring, and spa style bathrooms that elevate the guest experience.
Outdoor Integration

Arizona homeowners are connecting casitas to outdoor living areas with covered patios, sliding glass walls, and shaded seating. The indoor outdoor flow is a defining feature of modern desert architecture.
Energy Efficient Construction
Spray foam insulation, low emissivity windows, mini split climate systems, and solar ready roofs are becoming standard for casitas due to Arizona heat and long term utility savings.
Purpose Built Interior Layouts
Work From Home Studios
Soundproofing, built in shelving, natural light, and separate exterior entries help create a quiet environment for professionals working remotely.
Short Term Rental Ready
Casitas built with small kitchenettes, durable flooring, and separate HVAC controls can serve as rental units in areas where city guidelines allow it.
Wellness and Fitness Rooms
Some homeowners choose open floor plans for yoga, pilates, or personal gym use. High ceilings and natural light support this lifestyle driven design.
Creative and Hobby Spaces
Casitas also make ideal art studios, music rooms, or workshops that keep noise and clutter away from the main home.
Important Zoning and Legal Considerations in Arizona
City of Scottsdale Guidelines
Scottsdale regulates accessory structures based on lot size, height, and setbacks. Homeowners need to confirm whether a full kitchen is allowed and how large the casita can be on their property.
Phoenix Regulations
Phoenix allows accessory dwelling units in more zoning districts than in previous years, but requirements vary by neighborhood. Lot coverage limits and sewer tie ins often influence design.
Maricopa County Areas
Homes outside city limits may have different rules regarding casitas, including septic requirements, utility hookups, and maximum square footage.
Utility and Hookup Planning
Casitas require proper electrical, water, and sewer planning. Understanding these needs early helps avoid permitting delays and unexpected costs.
How to Start Your Casita Project with Confidence

Before starting your design, review your lot’s zoning, visit completed casita projects for inspiration, and consider how you want the space to function over the next decade. A smart casita design should balance privacy, comfort, and long term value while matching the architectural style of your home.

Casitas continue to be one of the most versatile and desirable additions Arizona homeowners can build. With thoughtful planning and modern design choices, your casita can serve as a flexible space that adapts to your lifestyle for years to come.

move-in-ready-vs-custom-build-pros-and-c.title: Move-In Ready vs Custom Build: Pros and Cons for Phoenix Buyers
move-in-ready-vs-custom-build-pros-and-c.description: Compare move-in ready and custom build homes in Phoenix and Scottsdale. Learn the pros and cons, timelines, cost considerations and how to choose the right home for your lifestyle.

blocks.text: Move-In Ready vs Custom Build: Pros and Cons for Phoenix Buyers

When you’re looking for a new home in Phoenix or Scottsdale, one of the first decisions you’ll face is whether to buy a move-in ready house or build a custom home from scratch. Each option comes with its own advantages and trade-offs, and the right choice depends on your budget, timeline and personal vision.
What is a move-in ready home?

A move-in ready (also known as spec) home is a house that is built by a developer or builder without a specific buyer lined up. These homes are finished or nearly finished and are ready for occupancy soon after purchase. Because they’re designed to appeal to a broad market, they often feature popular layouts and finishes that work for many families.
Benefits of move-in ready homes
Speed and convenience - You can close and move in quickly because construction and permitting are already complete. This is ideal if you’re relocating or need a home on a fixed timeline.
Predictable costs - The price is established up front, so there are no surprises or change orders during construction. You’ll know exactly what you’re paying and can secure financing accordingly.
Proven floor plans - Spec homes are often based on floor plans that have been refined over time and offer efficient use of space. You can walk through the home and see how it feels before committing.
Lower decision fatigue - With finishes and fixtures already chosen, you won’t spend weeks agonizing over every design detail.
Drawbacks of move-in ready homes
Limited personalization - Because the builder has already selected the design and materials, you have fewer opportunities to change floor plans or finishes.
Compromises on style - You may need to accept design elements that aren’t your favorite, such as cabinet colors or bathroom tile.
Competition from other buyers - The best spec homes can attract multiple offers, especially in desirable Phoenix neighborhoods.
What is a custom build?

A custom home is designed and built around your unique vision. Working with an architect and builder, you select every element - from the layout and exterior style to finishes, fixtures and smart-home systems. In Phoenix and Scottsdale, custom builds often incorporate desert-friendly design elements like shaded patios, large windows for views and energy-efficient materials.
Benefits of custom builds
Complete personalization - You have full control over the layout, materials and features. Want a spacious gourmet kitchen, attached casita or RV garage? You can design it from the ground up.

Quality and energy efficiency - Custom builders like Jematell Homes can source high-end materials and integrate features like passive solar orientation, smart home automation and insulation to cope with Arizona’s climate.
Long-term satisfaction - Because the home reflects your lifestyle and tastes, it may better suit your needs for years to come, reducing the urge to renovate or move later.
Distinctive design - A custom home stands out from cookie-cutter subdivisions, giving you a unique property in the valley.
Drawbacks of custom builds

Longer timeline - Designing, permitting and building from scratch can take many months or longer, which isn’t ideal if you need housing quickly. Construction schedules can also be impacted by material delays or permit approvals.
More decisions - Choosing every element of the home can be rewarding but also overwhelming. It helps to work with a builder who guides you through selections.
Phoenix considerations
Building or buying in the Phoenix metro area means thinking about local climate, lifestyle and zoning.

Climate - Summers are hot and dry, so energy-efficient design, insulation, and high SEER-rated HVAC systems are critical. Covered patios, misting systems and north-facing orientation can improve comfort.

Indoor-outdoor living - Desert living encourages blending interior spaces with the outdoors. Whether you choose a move-in ready or custom home, look for features like retractable walls, courtyards and shaded pools.

Future growth - Some buyers value the ability to add features later, such as a guest casita or workshop. Custom builds make this easier, but some spec homes may be built on larger lots for future additions.
Which option is right for you?
There’s no one-size-fits-all answer. A move-in ready home is ideal if you:
need to move quickly without the stress of building,
want predictable costs and timelines, and
are satisfied with popular layouts and finishes.
A custom build may be better if you:
have a specific vision that existing homes can’t satisfy,
are willing to invest time and budget into creating your dream home, and
value distinctive architecture and personalized features.

Whatever you choose, partnering with an experienced builder is key. Jematell Homes brings extensive local expertise in Arizona homebuilding, delivering both high-quality spec homes and fully custom builds tailored to each client’s vision. We can help you tour available move-in ready homes or start a design consultation for a custom build in Phoenix, Scottsdale or surrounding communities. Reach out today to explore your options and find the perfect fit for your lifestyle.

practical-steps-to-successful-custom-hom.title: Practical Steps to Successful Custom Home Design
practical-steps-to-successful-custom-hom.description: Are you ready to turn your dream home from a mere vision into reality? Today, we're going to explore the exciting world of custom home design. Follow these practical steps and you'll be well on your way to owning your personalized paradise.

blocks.text: Practical Steps to Successful Custom Home Design

Are you ready to turn your dream home from a mere vision into reality? Today, we're going to explore the exciting world of custom home design. Follow these practical steps and you'll be well on your way to owning your personalized paradise.
1. Define Your Dream Home Vision

When it comes to custom home design, the first step is crystal clear - define your dream home vision. Sounds simple, right? But, it's not just about picking out a fancy door or deciding on the color of the kitchen cabinets.

Start by identifying the overall style you want for your home. Is it a modern minimalist design you're after or perhaps a rustic farmhouse feel? Maybe you're longing for a classic colonial style or a sleek urban loft. Take time to explore various styles and decide which one resonates with your taste.

Next, think about functionality. How many bedrooms and bathrooms do you need? Do you want an open floor plan or separate, defined spaces? Do you dream of a large gourmet kitchen or do you prefer a cozy, compact cooking space? It's important to consider your lifestyle and the needs of your family during this phase of custom home design.

Then, consider the details. The beauty of custom home design lies in the ability to personalize every nook and cranny of your home. Do you want a fireplace in the living room? How about built-in bookshelves in the study? Even the smallest details like the type of door knobs can make a big difference in achieving your dream home.

Lastly, budget is key. Defining your dream home vision means being realistic about what you can afford. Remember, dream homes don't have to be mansions. Even a small, thoughtfully designed home can be your perfect sanctuary.

The journey to a successful custom home design begins with a well-defined vision. So, grab a notebook and start jotting down ideas. As you sketch out your dream, remember - it's not just about creating a house, it's about crafting your very own haven.
Stay tuned for our next step - selecting the perfect plot. Your dream home is just a few practical steps away!
2. Select the Perfect Plot

After defining the vision for your custom home design, it's time to take the next crucial step - choosing the perfect plot. Remember, the location of your home plays a huge role in your day-to-day life.

First off, consider the neighborhood. Do you prefer a bustling city or a quiet countryside? Maybe you're drawn to a suburban community or the laid-back vibe of a beach town. Think about what kind of surroundings would complement your lifestyle and your custom home design.

Next, pay attention to the size and shape of the plot. Will it accommodate your home design? Is there enough room for a garden or a backyard pool? Don't forget to consider the orientation of your lot - where will the sun rise and set in relation to your home?

Think about accessibility and convenience. How close is the plot to essential amenities like schools, hospitals, and grocery stores? What about proximity to your place of work or public transportation?

Lastly, explore the legalities. Are there any zoning restrictions or building codes that could affect your custom home design? Be sure to do your homework to avoid potential legal hiccups down the line.

Choosing the perfect plot requires careful thought and consideration. It's not just about finding a piece of land - it's about finding a place that feels like home. After all, your custom home design deserves the perfect backdrop.
Ready for the next step? Stay tuned as we dive into collaborating with a trusted architect. Your dream home is getting closer to reality!
3. Collaborate with a Home Builder

After selecting the perfect plot, your next adventure on this custom home design journey is partnering with a trusted Home Builder. This professional will be instrumental in transforming your dream vision into a tangible design, so it's essential to pick the right individual or team.

Discuss your Budget: Let's not beat around the bush - building a custom home design can be pricey. Talk about your budget early on to ensure the architect can design within your financial boundaries. Transparency here can save you both from unpleasant surprises later.

Check their Credentials: Confirm that the architect is licensed and registered. It's also a good idea to check if they have any professional affiliations or awards. This can give you an added assurance of their credibility and expertise.
References: Get in touch with former clients if possible. Their experiences and reviews can give you a real sense of what it's like to work with the architect.

Remember, finding the right architect is like finding the right partner - it might take some time and patience, but the right fit can make all the difference in your custom home design journey. It's the collaboration of your dreams and their expertise that's going to result in the home you've always wanted.
Next up, we'll navigate the exciting world of the construction process. Your dream home is a few steps closer to becoming a reality!
4. Navigate the Construction Process

With your dream home design and an architect on board, it's time to roll up your sleeves and dive into the construction process. This phase can be a whirlwind of activity, but with a bit of preparation, you can minimize stress and stay on top of things.

Assemble Your Dream Team: Beyond your trusted architect, you'll need a skilled builder who understands your vision and can execute your custom home design. Be sure to look for a builder who has experience with custom homes and comes with solid recommendations.

Know Your Timeline: Construction is a marathon, not a sprint. Discuss the timeline with your builder and architect, and make sure to factor in potential delays due to weather, material availability, or permit issues. Patience is key here, and it pays to remember that quality can't be rushed.

Stay Involved: Regular site visits can help you stay connected with your project. This gives you a chance to see the progress, ask questions, and address any concerns promptly. But remember, too much micromanagement can hinder the work of your professionals - trust their expertise!

Prepare for Changes: No matter how well you plan, unexpected changes can occur. It could be anything from finding unstable soil to deciding you want larger windows in your living room. Flexibility and open communication can help you navigate these changes without losing sight of your custom home design.

Celebrate Milestones: Each completed phase of construction is a step closer to your dream home. Celebrate these moments - it's a reminder of the progress you're making and can be a great morale booster during the process.

And just like that, you're well on your way to unlocking your dream home! Navigating the construction process of your custom home design can be an exhilarating journey filled with ups and downs, but the end result - a home designed just for you - makes it all worth it.
5. Your Dream Home Awaits
Building a custom home is a journey unlike any other. It's an opportunity to create a space that is uniquely yours, where memories will be made and cherished for years to come.

Whether you're looking for an affordable custom home or a luxury dream home, we're here to help. With our expertise in custom home building and our commitment to quality and craftsmanship, we're confident that we'll deliver a home that you'll love for a lifetime.
Start your custom home building journey today and let us be your trusted partner every step of the way.

questions-to-ask-custom-home-builder-ari.title: Questions to Ask a Custom Home Builder Before You Sign
questions-to-ask-custom-home-builder-ari.description: Before hiring a custom home builder in Arizona, ask these questions. What to look for in contracts, timelines, experience, and communication before you commit.

blocks.text: Questions to Ask a Custom Home Builder Before You Sign Anything

Choosing a custom home builder is one of the most consequential decisions in the entire build process. The wrong choice does not just cost money. It costs time, creates stress, and can result in a home that does not match what you were promised. The right questions asked before you sign a contract surface the information you need to make a confident decision. This list covers what to ask, what good answers look like, and what should give you pause.
Have You Built Homes in This Area Before?

Builder experience in a specific city or jurisdiction matters more than most buyers realize. Permitting in Scottsdale operates differently than permitting in Mesa, Gilbert, Chandler, or Maricopa County's unincorporated areas. A builder who knows the local permit office, has established relationships with inspectors, and understands the jurisdiction-specific requirements will move faster and run into fewer surprises than one who is learning on your project.

Ask for examples of homes built in your specific city. Ask how many permits they have pulled with that jurisdiction in the last two years. The answers reveal whether local experience is genuine or just claimed.
What Does Your Contract Include and What Does It Not?
A contract that reads well on the surface can still leave you exposed if key items are excluded from the scope. Before you sign, get clear answers on the following.

Is site work included? Grading, clearing, utility connections, and access road preparation are often excluded from base contracts and priced separately. If you are building on your own lot in a semi-rural area of Maricopa County, Rio Verde, or anywhere outside an established subdivision, site work costs can be significant.
Are permits included? Some builders include permit fees in their contract price. Others pass them through as a separate cost. Know which applies before you sign.

What is the allowance structure? Custom and semi-custom homes often use allowances for finishes. If your selections exceed the allowance, the difference becomes a change order. Understanding allowance amounts before signing protects you from surprises mid-build.
How Do You Handle Change Orders?

Change orders are modifications to the original scope of work after a contract is signed. They are common in custom home building, and how a builder manages them tells you a lot about how the project will feel to live through.

Ask what triggers a change order. Ask how quickly changes are priced and presented for approval. Ask whether construction continues during the change order review process or pauses until you sign off. A builder with a clear, documented change order process is a builder who operates transparently. A builder who is vague about the process is one who may use change orders as a margin-recovery tool later in the project.
What Is a Realistic Timeline for My Build?

Every buyer wants to hear a fast timeline. A good builder gives you an honest one. Ask for a project schedule broken into phases: design and permitting, site preparation, foundation, framing, mechanical rough-ins, drywall, finishes, and final inspection. Ask what factors could extend each phase and what has caused delays on recent projects.

In the Phoenix metro, permit timelines vary by city. Mesa typically processes residential plan reviews in 3 to 18 business days. Scottsdale runs 10 to 15 business days for a first review. Maricopa County unincorporated areas, which includes Rio Verde, can run 5 to 30 days. A builder who does not account for jurisdiction-specific permit timelines in their schedule is giving you a timeline that does not reflect reality.
Who Will Be My Day-to-Day Contact During Construction?

On many custom home builds, the person who sells you the project is not the person who manages it. Knowing who you will actually be communicating with during construction matters. Ask who your project manager is, how often they are on site, and how they prefer to communicate updates.

Ask how disputes or concerns are handled if they arise during construction. A builder with a clear escalation path and a committed project manager is a builder who treats communication as part of the service, not an afterthought. If the answer is vague or if the salesperson deflects the question, that is worth noting before you sign.
Can I See Recent Projects and Speak With Past Clients?
A portfolio of finished homes tells you about design capability. A conversation with a past client tells you about the experience of working with the builder. Both matter.

Ask for references from projects completed in the last 12 to 24 months, ideally in a similar price range and scope to your build. When you speak with those references, ask specifically about communication during construction, how change orders were handled, whether the final cost came in close to the original contract, and whether they would build with the same builder again.
A builder who is reluctant to provide recent references or who offers only dated ones is a builder whose recent track record may not hold up to scrutiny.
What Warranties Do You Provide?

Arizona law requires builders to provide certain implied warranties on new construction, but the specifics of what a builder offers beyond the legal minimum vary significantly. Ask for the warranty documentation in writing before you sign anything.

At minimum, understand the coverage for structural defects, workmanship, and mechanical systems. Ask whether the builder uses manufacturer warranties on major components like HVAC, roofing, and windows, and how warranty claims are submitted and resolved after closing.
A builder who is proud of their work is a builder who stands behind it clearly. Warranty terms that are hard to get in writing are a signal worth paying attention to.
Are You Licensed, Bonded, and Insured in Arizona?

This is a non-negotiable baseline. Arizona requires home builders to be licensed through the Arizona Registrar of Contractors. Verify the license independently at roc.az.gov rather than taking the builder's word for it. Confirm they carry general liability insurance and workers' compensation coverage.
Building with an unlicensed contractor in Arizona eliminates your recourse through the Registrar of Contractors if something goes wrong. It is a risk that is easy to avoid and carries no upside.
What Makes You the Right Builder for My Specific Project?

This is the question that separates builders who are genuinely a fit from those who are simply available. A builder who has done significant work in Rio Verde understands the infrastructure variables that come with that area. A builder with experience in Scottsdale knows the city's design review expectations. A builder who has worked in Mesa, Gilbert, Chandler, and Maricopa has a breadth of jurisdiction experience that reduces risk across different project types.

Listen for specificity. A builder who can speak concretely about your project type, your location, and your budget is a builder who has thought about whether they are the right fit. A builder who gives you a generic answer is one who is selling rather than advising.

At Jematell Homes, we build custom and semi-custom homes across the Phoenix metro including Scottsdale, Mesa, Gilbert, Chandler, Maricopa, Queen Creek, and Rio Verde. We are glad to answer every question on this list and put the answers in writing.
Frequently Asked Questions
How do I verify a home builder's license in Arizona?

Visit roc.az.gov and search by the builder's name or license number. Arizona home builders are required to hold a license through the Arizona Registrar of Contractors. Always verify independently rather than relying on documentation provided by the builder.
What should a custom home contract include?

At minimum, a contract should include a detailed scope of work, allowance amounts for finish selections, a payment schedule tied to construction milestones, a change order process, the project timeline, and warranty terms. Any item not in writing is an item that may become a disagreement later.
How much do custom homes cost in Arizona?

Cost varies significantly by location, lot conditions, square footage, and finish level. Land costs in Scottsdale and Paradise Valley run higher than in Mesa, Gilbert, Chandler, or Maricopa. Per-square-foot construction costs are broadly consistent across the Phoenix metro. A realistic budget conversation with your builder before design begins is the most direct way to understand your total project cost.
Does Jematell Homes build custom homes in Scottsdale, Mesa, Gilbert, and Chandler?

Yes. Jematell Homes builds custom and semi-custom homes across the Phoenix metro including Scottsdale, Mesa, Gilbert, Chandler, Maricopa, Queen Creek, and Rio Verde. Contact us to discuss your project and get direct answers to every question on this list.
What is the difference between a custom home and a semi-custom home?

A custom home starts from scratch with your lot, your architect, and a floor plan built around your specific requirements. A semi-custom home uses an established floor plan as the starting point and gives you meaningful personalization within that framework. Semi-custom typically costs less and delivers faster than a fully custom build.

rio-verde-arizona-utilities-water-infras.title: Rio Verde AZ Utilities & Water: What Buyers Must Know
rio-verde-arizona-utilities-water-infras.description: Planning to build a custom home in Rio Verde, AZ? Learn what you need to know about water access, EPCOR service areas, and infrastructure costs before you buy land.

blocks.text: Rio Verde, AZ Utilities and Water Access: What Custom Home Buyers Need to Know Before They Build

Rio Verde is one of the most compelling places to build a custom home in the Phoenix metro. Golf course lots, Sonoran Desert views, and a pace of life that larger cities cannot replicate. But before you commit to land in Rio Verde, you need to understand the utility infrastructure, particularly water. It is more nuanced than most buyers expect, and the details directly affect your budget, your timeline, and the long-term livability of your home.
Rio Verde Proper vs. Rio Verde Foothills: The Distinction That Changes Everything

Rio Verde is not a single unified municipality with a single utility provider. It is an unincorporated community in Maricopa County, and the infrastructure landscape splits into two distinctly different areas: Rio Verde proper and Rio Verde Foothills (also referred to as Rio Verde Highlands).

Which area your land sits in determines everything about your water access, your utility costs, and your path to a building permit. This is the first question to get answered, before you make an offer on any parcel.
Water in Rio Verde Proper: Established EPCOR Service

In the core Rio Verde community, water and wastewater service is managed by EPCOR Arizona. EPCOR acquired Rio Verde Utilities Inc. in 2019 and currently serves approximately 1,000 connections in the established community. For buyers building on lots within Rio Verde proper, this is a relatively straightforward utility situation, a regulated private utility with an established connection process.

Budget for standard EPCOR connection fees and service initiation when building in this area. Your builder and civil engineer can determine the specific costs based on your lot's location and proximity to existing water lines. In most cases, this is a predictable line item, not a wildcard.
Water in Rio Verde Foothills: The Standpipe Solution

The Foothills area has a more complicated history. For years, many residents and builders relied on water hauled from Scottsdale. In January 2023, Scottsdale ended that service, leaving Rio Verde Foothills without a reliable water source and making national headlines.

That situation has now reached a resolution. EPCOR invested more than $10 million to build a dedicated pipeline and standpipe filling station for Rio Verde Foothills, on 176th Street south of Rio Verde Drive, and operates daily from 7 a.m. to 6 p.m. Residents fill water trucks at the standpipe and haul water to their homes. Access requires a customer account and PIN tied to a property within the designated service area.

This is a meaningful step forward from the crisis of 2023, but it is not the same as having a piped water connection directly to your home. Buyers need to understand that distinction before purchasing land in the Foothills.
The Growth Cap: Why This Matters for New Construction

This is the detail that most buyers and even some real estate professionals overlook: the EPCOR Foothills standpipe system is capped at 150 additional homes. Once that limit is reached, new construction in the Foothills will face significant water access challenges with no clear alternative currently in place.

If you are evaluating raw land in Rio Verde Foothills, confirm directly with EPCOR or through your builder whether standpipe connections are still available for that parcel. This is not a minor administrative step, it determines whether the lot is buildable under current infrastructure conditions.

Water costs in the Foothills also run higher than in Rio Verde proper. Residents are funding the $10 million project through their water bills, with rates running double or triple what comparable EPCOR customers in the established community pay. Factor that into your long-term operating budget, not just your build budget.
Wastewater and Septic in Rio Verde

Much of Rio Verde, including both the established community and the Foothills, operates on septic systems rather than municipal sewer. If your lot requires a new septic installation, budget $15,000 to $35,000 depending on soil conditions, lot size, and system type. Maricopa County Environmental Services regulates septic installations and requires a percolation test before permitting.

Soil conditions in parts of Rio Verde can complicate septic installation and push costs higher. A builder with experience in the area will flag this during early site evaluation rather than letting it surface as a surprise mid-project.
Electric Service in Rio Verde

APS (Arizona Public Service) provides electric service throughout Rio Verde and the surrounding areas. New construction requires coordination with APS for service extension to the lot. In more remote sections of Rio Verde and the Foothills, extending power lines can add meaningful cost depending on the distance from existing infrastructure.

Request a service extension estimate from APS early in your due diligence - ideally before you close on the land. In established East Valley cities like Mesa, Gilbert, Chandler, and Scottsdale , power infrastructure is typically already at the lot. In Rio Verde, that assumption does not always hold.
Internet and Connectivity

Connectivity in Rio Verde varies by exact location. Cable and fiber service is limited or unavailable in parts of the community, particularly in the Foothills. Starlink satellite internet has become a practical and widely used solution for residents in these areas.

If remote work, video conferencing, or high-bandwidth use is part of your daily routine, confirm service availability at the specific parcel address before finalizing your land purchase. This is a quality-of-life detail that deserves the same due diligence as any other infrastructure item.
How Utilities Affect Your Rio Verde Custom Home Budget

Utility infrastructure is one of the most frequently underestimated cost categories in rural and semi-rural custom home builds. In established cities across the Phoenix metro, utility connections are straightforward and largely predictable. In Rio Verde, particularly in the Foothills; the infrastructure situation adds complexity that needs to be addressed in the early budget, not discovered at permitting.
A realistic Rio Verde custom home build budget should include dedicated line items for:
Water access costs (standpipe service fees or EPCOR connection fees depending on location)
Septic system design, permitting, and installation
APS electric service extension if the lot does not have existing power
Any road, grading, or easement work required to access the parcel

The custom home building process looks different in Rio Verde than it does in Scottsdale or the East Valley. Understanding that upfront is what separates a smooth build from one that stalls at permitting.
The Bottom Line for Rio Verde Buyers

Rio Verde is worth building in. The lifestyle, the lot character, and the views are genuinely difficult to replicate elsewhere in the Phoenix metro. But the infrastructure reality is materially different from what buyers encounter in established suburban cities, and the Foothills adds another layer of complexity specific to water access and growth limitations.

Know which area your land sits in. Understand the water situation specific to that parcel. Get your utility questions answered before you close. And work with a builder who has built in this area and knows what the infrastructure picture looks like before it becomes a problem.

At Jematell Homes, we build custom homes in Rio Verde and across the Phoenix metro. If you are evaluating land and want direct answers about what it will cost and what the process looks like, start the conversation here.
You might also find these helpful as you evaluate Rio Verde:

Lot Considerations Most Buyers Miss in Rio Verde
Rio Verde Floor Plans That Live Well
Rio Verde: Lot Fit, Views, and HOA Reality Check
Frequently Asked Questions

Does Rio Verde have city water? No. Rio Verde is an unincorporated community without municipal water service. The established community is served by EPCOR Arizona. Rio Verde Foothills is served by an EPCOR standpipe filling station that opened January 1, 2026. Neither operates as a traditional piped city water connection.

Can I still build a home in Rio Verde Foothills? It depends on whether standpipe connections remain available. EPCOR's current Foothills system is capped at 150 additional homes. Confirm availability before making any land purchase decision in the Foothills area.

How does building in Rio Verde compare to building in Scottsdale or Mesa? The home build itself follows similar processes and quality standards. The main difference is the infrastructure situation - utilities, septic, and lot access require more due diligence in Rio Verde than in established East Valley cities like Mesa, Gilbert, Chandler, or Maricopa where infrastructure is already in place at most lots.
Who provides electricity in Rio Verde, AZ? APS (Arizona Public Service) provides electric service throughout Rio Verde and the surrounding areas.

Does Jematell Homes build custom homes in Rio Verde? Yes. Jematell Homes builds custom and semi-custom homes in Rio Verde and across the Phoenix metro including Scottsdale, Mesa, Gilbert, Chandler, and Maricopa. rio-verde-floor-plans-desert-living.title: Rio Verde Floor Plans for Desert Living
rio-verde-floor-plans-desert-living.description: Explore Rio Verde floor plans designed for desert living. Learn how courtyards, guest suites, shaded outdoor spaces, and heat-smart layouts create homes that perform beautifully in Arizona.

blocks.text: Rio Verde Floor Plans That Live Well: Courtyards, Guest Space, and Desert-Proof Layouts

Looking for Rio Verde floor plans that truly fit desert living? This guide explores courtyard concepts, guest-friendly layouts, heat-conscious design, and smart storage solutions built specifically for the Rio Verde lifestyle.
Floor plans in Rio Verde are not just about square footage.
They are about flow, comfort, privacy, and performance in a true desert environment. The right layout should respond to the land, the climate, and how homeowners actually live here.
If you’re searching for Rio Verde floor plans, here’s what to prioritize.
Courtyard-Centered Living
Courtyard layouts work exceptionally well in Rio Verde.

They create private outdoor living without relying on fencing or tall walls. In communities where open desert views matter, a well-positioned courtyard allows you to enjoy natural light while maintaining privacy from neighboring homes.
Courtyards also:
Buffer harsh afternoon sun
Provide shaded outdoor space
Improve cross-ventilation
Create a focal point for interior sightlines
Rather than placing all glass along the rear elevation, a courtyard plan distributes light more intentionally and reduces excessive heat gain.
When reviewing our Floor Plans, consider how adaptable each design is to a courtyard configuration based on lot orientation.
Guest Space That Feels Intentional
Many Rio Verde homeowners value flexible guest accommodations.

Whether hosting seasonal visitors or family, guest suites should feel integrated but separate. A well-designed floor plan positions guest bedrooms away from primary suites while maintaining easy access to shared living areas.
Look for:
Split-wing layouts
Private bath access
Clear traffic flow that avoids cutting through bedrooms
Outdoor access from guest areas where appropriate
This creates a home that functions comfortably year-round, not just during short visits.

If you’re comparing home types, understanding the differences outlined in Spec Home vs Custom Home vs Semi-Custom: Finding Your Perfect Fit in Arizona can help clarify how much flexibility you want in tailoring guest spaces.
Shaded Outdoor Living That Actually Work
Outdoor living is central to Rio Verde, but it must be designed thoughtfully.
Deep covered patios, extended roof overhangs, and transitional indoor-outdoor spaces allow you to enjoy the desert climate without being overwhelmed by it.
Effective Rio Verde floor plans consider:
West-facing exposure
Overhang depth relative to window height
Placement of outdoor kitchens or fireplaces
Sightlines from interior gathering spaces
A shaded outdoor area should feel like a natural extension of the great room, not an afterthought.
Keeping the Heat Out: Smart Desert Layout Strategy
In Rio Verde, orientation is everything.

Floor plans should place primary living spaces where they can benefit from morning light and avoid prolonged western sun exposure. Bedrooms positioned along extreme heat walls can increase cooling demand and reduce comfort.
Desert-aware layouts often include:
Buffer zones such as hallways or storage along west walls
Fewer large windows facing direct afternoon sun
Strategic window placement to frame views without overheating interiors
Energy performance begins with layout decisions long before insulation or mechanical systems are installed.
If you want to avoid costly missteps, reviewing How to Avoid Common Mistakes When Building a Custom Home in Arizona can help align your layout decisions with long-term performance goals.
Storage, Sightlines, and Everyday Function
Rio Verde living often means more than just entertaining.

Homes need practical storage for seasonal items, outdoor gear, and long-term organization. Floor plans that incorporate walk-in pantries, functional mudroom transitions, and intentional storage zones tend to age better over time.
Sightlines also matter.
When you enter a home, what do you see? A clear visual connection to outdoor views creates a sense of openness, while thoughtful separation of private spaces maintains comfort.
A well-designed custom layout balances open concept living with subtle spatial definition.
Choosing a Rio Verde Floor Plan That Fits Your Lot
The best floor plan on paper may not work on your specific parcel.
Lot dimensions, setbacks, slope, and view corridors all influence how a layout should be adapted. That’s why evaluating floor plans alongside lot characteristics is essential.

Before finalizing a layout, compare your lot’s setbacks, slope, and view corridors against adaptable Floor Plans to ensure the design truly fits the land rather than forcing the land to fit the design.
When reviewing our Custom Homes process, you’ll see how site-specific adjustments transform a standard plan into something truly tailored to Rio Verde living.
Final Thoughts: Floor Plans Designed for Rio Verde Living
A strong Rio Verde floor plan does more than look impressive in renderings.
It protects you from heat.
It supports how you host guests.
It creates private outdoor moments.
It aligns with your lot’s views and orientation.

rio-verde-vs-scottsdale-custom-home.title: Rio Verde vs. Scottsdale: Where to Build Your Custom Home
rio-verde-vs-scottsdale-custom-home.description: Rio Verde and Scottsdale both attract custom home buyers in Arizona. Here is how to compare lot costs, lifestyle, permitting, and long-term value before you decide.

blocks.text: Rio Verde vs. Scottsdale: Which Location Makes More Sense for Your Custom Build?

Two of the most popular areas for custom home builds in the Phoenix metro sit less than 30 minutes apart, but they deliver meaningfully different experiences. Rio Verde and Scottsdale attract similar buyers, people who want space, quality, and something built specifically for them, but the decision between the two comes down to priorities that only you can rank. This breakdown covers the factors that matter most: land, lifestyle, costs, permitting, and long-term value.
The Core Lifestyle Difference

Scottsdale is a city with full municipal infrastructure, dense amenities, and a well-established luxury real estate market. Building a custom home in Scottsdale, particularly in North Scottsdale, puts you close to premier dining, retail, healthcare, and entertainment without sacrificing the quality of the build.

Rio Verde is an unincorporated community in Maricopa County. It is quieter, more spacious, and more removed from the urban core. Residents trade proximity for lot character, larger parcels, stronger desert views, and a pace of life that Scottsdale's more developed areas simply cannot replicate.
Neither is better. They serve different versions of the same buyer.
Lot Size and Land Cost

This is where Rio Verde pulls ahead for buyers who want space. Lots in Rio Verde regularly run from one to several acres, and per-acre land costs are meaningfully lower than comparable Scottsdale parcels. In North Scottsdale, premium custom home lots can exceed $500,000 for land alone before a single foundation is poured. In Rio Verde, you can find larger parcels at a fraction of that cost.

The tradeoff is infrastructure. In Scottsdale, that lot price often comes with utilities already at the property line. In Rio Verde, particularly in the Foothill, you may be responsible for utility extension, septic installation, and access road work. We covered the full infrastructure picture in detail in our Rio Verde utilities guide. Factor those costs into your land comparison before assuming Rio Verde is automatically the better value.
Permitting and Jurisdiction
This is a practical difference that affects your build timeline and who you are working with throughout the process.

Scottsdale custom home permits go through the City of Scottsdale's Planning and Development department. First plan review typically takes 10 to 15 business days, and the city has its own set of design standards, zoning requirements, and inspection processes. Scottsdale's permitting system is well-established and builder-familiar, which generally means fewer surprises.

Rio Verde, as an unincorporated community, falls under Maricopa County. Permitting goes through Maricopa County's Building and Planning department. Review timelines run 5 to 30 days depending on project complexity. The county process is less prescriptive than Scottsdale's in some ways, which can offer more flexibility on design, but it also means your builder needs to know the county's specific requirements well. Gaps in that knowledge can create delays.
Working with a builder who has direct experience in both Scottsdale and Rio Verde is the most direct way to avoid permitting friction on either side.
HOA Rules and Community Governance
Both markets have HOA-governed communities, but the specifics differ significantly by location.

In Scottsdale, HOAs tend to be more standardized and expectations around design, materials, and exterior appearance are often clearly documented. Many North Scottsdale custom home communities have architectural review committees that must approve plans before permits are pulled.

In Rio Verde, HOA rules vary depending on whether you are building within an established golf or gated community or on unincorporated land in the Foothills. The Rio Verde Country Club community has its own standards. The Foothills areas may have less formal governance but can still carry deed restrictions that affect what you can build. Know the specific rules on any parcel before you close.
We covered the HOA reality in Rio Verde in an earlier post if you want the deeper breakdown.
Commute and Access

Scottsdale custom home builds in areas like DC Ranch, Grayhawk, or McDowell Mountain put you 20 to 40 minutes from Sky Harbor, close to the Loop 101, and within easy reach of Chandler, Gilbert, Mesa, and the broader East Valley.

Rio Verde sits further north and east. Depending on exactly where your lot is, you are looking at 30 to 50 minutes to reach central Scottsdale, and longer to reach the East Valley cities of Gilbert, Chandler, or Mesa. For buyers who work remotely or have flexible schedules, this is a non-issue. For buyers who commute daily or need frequent access to Phoenix's urban core, it matters.

The connectivity question also extends to internet service. Scottsdale has full fiber and cable infrastructure across most of its custom home areas. Parts of Rio Verde rely on satellite internet, particularly in the Foothills. If your work depends on reliable, high-speed connectivity, confirm service at the specific parcel before you build.
Home Values and Investment Trajectory

Both markets have shown strong appreciation. Rio Verde's median home value reached approximately $877,000 as of early 2026, with average sale prices pushing past $960,000. Scottsdale's custom home market spans a wider range, with entry points in the upper $700,000s and no real ceiling in communities like Paradise Valley adjacent areas or guard-gated North Scottsdale.

Scottsdale carries stronger name recognition in national real estate markets and consistent long-term demand driven by job growth, corporate relocations, and the broader Phoenix metro expansion. Rio Verde's value trajectory is tied more closely to quality of life demand and the relative scarcity of large-lot, rural-adjacent properties near a major metro.

Both are sound markets for a well-built custom home. The question is whether you are optimizing for resale liquidity or lifestyle quality, and whether those two things point to the same answer for you.
Which One Is Right for You

Scottsdale tends to be the right fit if proximity to amenities and commute efficiency matter, if you want a larger pool of comparables supporting your home's value, or if you prefer a more established infrastructure and permitting environment.

Rio Verde tends to be the right fit if lot size and land character are the priority, if a quieter and more removed lifestyle is genuinely what you are after, and if the infrastructure considerations are manageable given your specific parcel situation.

In many cases, the answer becomes clear once you have actually walked both types of lots. The experience of standing on a one-acre Rio Verde parcel with desert and mountain views is different from standing on a half-acre Scottsdale lot in a master-planned community, and one of them will feel more like home before you have drawn a single floor plan.

At Jematell Homes, we build custom and semi-custom homes in both markets and across the Phoenix metro including Mesa, Gilbert, Chandler, Maricopa, and Paradise Valley. If you are evaluating both locations and want a straight conversation about what your budget realistically builds in each area, we are glad to have it.

Frequently Asked Questions
Is Rio Verde part of Scottsdale?

No. Rio Verde is an unincorporated community in Maricopa County, not within the city limits of Scottsdale. It sits northeast of Scottsdale near Fountain Hills. The two areas are distinct in terms of jurisdiction, permitting, utilities, and governance.
How far is Rio Verde from Scottsdale?
Rio Verde is approximately 25 to 35 miles from central Scottsdale depending on your exact destination, translating to roughly 30 to 50 minutes of drive time.
Is it cheaper to build a custom home in Rio Verde than Scottsdale?

Land costs in Rio Verde are generally lower than comparable Scottsdale parcels, particularly in North Scottsdale. However, infrastructure costs, utilities, septic, access, can be higher in Rio Verde, especially in the Foothills. A true cost comparison needs to account for both land price and infrastructure requirements.
Does Jematell Homes build in both Rio Verde and Scottsdale?

Yes. Jematell Homes builds custom and semi-custom homes in Rio Verde, Scottsdale, and across the Phoenix metro including Mesa, Gilbert, Chandler, and Maricopa. Contact us to discuss your land and build goals in either market.
What is the permitting process for a custom home in Rio Verde?

Rio Verde falls under Maricopa County's jurisdiction since it is unincorporated. Permitting goes through the Maricopa County Planning and Development department. Review timelines vary from 5 to 30 days depending on project scope and complexity.

semi-custom-homes-arizona.title: Semi-Custom Homes in Arizona: What You Can and Cannot Change
semi-custom-homes-arizona.description: A semi-custom home gives you real personalization without the full custom process. Learn what you can change, what is fixed, and whether it is the right fit for you in Arizona.

blocks.text: Semi-Custom Homes in Arizona: What You Can Personalize and What's Already Decided

The term semi-custom gets used loosely in the home building industry, and that creates confusion for buyers trying to figure out what they are actually signing up for. Some builders use it to describe a spec home with a few finish options. Others use it to describe something much closer to a fully custom build with an established floor plan as the starting point. Understanding exactly where on that spectrum your builder operates, and what that means for your decisions, is what this post covers.
Where Semi-Custom Sits in the Build Spectrum

There are three common paths to a new construction home in Arizona. A spec home is already built or well into construction before you buy it. You are choosing a finished product, not making build decisions. A fully custom home starts with a blank page, your lot, your architect, your floor plan, your everything. A semi-custom home sits between them. The floor plan framework is established, but you have meaningful input on how the interior takes shape.
That middle position is appealing to a specific type of buyer: someone who wants their home to feel personal without managing the full complexity and timeline of a ground-up custom build.
What You Can Personalize in a Semi-Custom Home
The personalization available in a semi-custom home typically covers the decisions that make the most difference in how a home feels day to day. These include:
Interior Finishes

Flooring material and color, cabinetry style and finish, countertop material and edge profile, tile selections for bathrooms and kitchen backsplash, fixture finishes throughout, these are the choices that define the visual identity of your home and are almost always fully in your hands in a semi-custom build.
Paint and Color Palette

Interior paint colors, exterior paint selections where applicable, and trim color are standard personalization options. These are low-cost decisions relative to their impact on the overall feel of the home.
Appliances and Mechanical Selections

Many semi-custom builders offer appliance packages at different price points, allowing you to select your range, refrigerator, dishwasher, and sometimes HVAC system within approved specifications. This matters in Arizona where HVAC performance directly affects long-term comfort and operating costs.
Fixture and Hardware Choices

Plumbing fixtures, door hardware, lighting fixtures, and ceiling fan selections are commonly part of the semi-custom allowance structure. Depending on your builder, these may come as packages or as individual selections with a set allowance amount.
Structural Options Within the Plan

Some semi-custom builders allow limited structural modifications, converting a bedroom to a home office with built-ins, extending a covered patio, adding a casita, or adjusting the primary suite layout. The degree of flexibility varies by builder and by how far along the plan is in the permit and engineering process. Earlier you get involved, the more structural flexibility typically exists.
What Is Already Decided
This is where buyers sometimes feel surprised after signing. In a semi-custom build, the following elements are generally fixed before you arrive.
The Overall Floor Plan and Footprint

The square footage, general room layout, ceiling heights, and structural envelope are established. You are working within a plan that has already been engineered and is often already permitted. Major floor plan changes, moving load-bearing walls, relocating plumbing stacks, changing rooflines, are either not available or come with significant cost and timeline consequences.
Lot Placement and Orientation

Where the home sits on the lot, how it faces, and how it relates to neighboring structures is determined before buyer selections begin. In Arizona, this matters more than most buyers initially appreciate, a poorly oriented home in the Phoenix metro means fighting afternoon sun in your main living areas and patio for decades.
Structural and Mechanical Systems

Foundation type, framing method, roof structure, and the routing of major mechanical systems are locked in at the engineering phase. These are not decisions you influence in a semi-custom build. What matters is that your builder has made good decisions on your behalf before you get to the table.
Exterior Architecture

The architectural style of the home, whether it is a modern desert design, a transitional farmhouse, or a traditional elevation, is typically fixed. You may have minor exterior material choices, but the overall look of the home from the street is set.
The Cost and Timeline Advantage
A semi-custom home generally costs less than a fully custom build and delivers faster. There are two reasons for this.

First, design costs are lower. A fully custom home requires an architect to produce plans from scratch, an engineer to review and stamp them, and a builder to price an entirely novel scope of work. A semi-custom home starts with an established, permitted plan. That design investment has already been made and is spread across multiple builds rather than charged to a single buyer.

Second, the builder knows the plan. When a builder has built a floor plan multiple times, they have established supplier relationships, refined their subcontractor scheduling, and resolved the problems that only surface during construction. That experience translates to fewer surprises and a more predictable timeline.

In Arizona's market, where fully custom homes in Scottsdale, Paradise Valley, or North Phoenix can take 14 to 22 months from design to move-in, a semi-custom build in Mesa, Gilbert, Chandler, or Scottsdale can realistically deliver a personalized home in 10 to 14 months from selections to close.
Who Semi-Custom Is the Right Fit For

Semi-custom works well for buyers who have a clear sense of their style and want their home to reflect it, but do not need to design the floor plan from scratch. It works for buyers who are working within a defined budget and want to maximize what they get within it. It works for buyers who have a timeline in mind and want a more predictable path than a full custom build typically provides.

It is less suited to buyers who have highly specific spatial requirements, an unusually large primary closet, a workshop that opens to the garage, a dedicated music room with acoustic wall treatment, that a standard plan cannot accommodate. Those buyers are better served by a fully custom process even if it takes longer and costs more, because the end result will actually match what they need.

At Jematell Homes, we offer both semi-custom and fully custom builds across the Phoenix metro, including Scottsdale, Mesa, Gilbert, Chandler, Maricopa, and Rio Verde. The right path depends on what you are building toward. If you are not sure which fits your situation, that is a conversation worth having before you commit to anything.
Frequently Asked Questions
What is the difference between a semi-custom and a custom home?

A custom home starts with a blank slate, your lot, your architect, your floor plan. A semi-custom home uses an established floor plan as the foundation and gives you meaningful personalization within that framework. Semi-custom typically costs less and delivers faster.
Can I change the floor plan in a semi-custom home?

Minor modifications are sometimes possible depending on where the project is in the design and permitting process. Major structural changes, moving walls, relocating plumbing, altering rooflines, are generally not available or carry significant added cost. The earlier you engage with the builder, the more flexibility you have.
Are semi-custom homes available in Scottsdale, Mesa, Gilbert, and Chandler?

Yes. Jematell Homes builds semi-custom and custom homes across the Phoenix metro including Scottsdale, Mesa, Gilbert, Chandler, Maricopa, and Rio Verde. Location availability depends on current lots and projects in each area.
How long does a semi-custom home take to build in Arizona?

Semi-custom builds typically run 10 to 14 months from selections to close, though timelines vary based on project scope, permitting timelines, and current construction demand. This is generally faster than a fully custom build.
What allowances are typical in a semi-custom home?

Allowances vary by builder and project. Common allowance categories include flooring, cabinetry, countertops, plumbing fixtures, lighting, and appliances. Understanding your allowance amounts before you sign is important, selection costs that exceed the allowance become out-of-pocket change orders.

spec-home-vs-custom-home-vs-semi-custom-.description: Explore the differences between spec, custom, and semi-custom homes in Arizona. Learn which home type best fits your lifestyle, budget, and timeline with guidance from Jematell Homes’ design-build experts in Phoenix and Scottsdale.

blocks.text: Spec Home vs Custom Home vs Semi-Custom: Finding Your Perfect Fit in Arizona

Choosing the right path for your new home is one of the most important decisions you’ll make when building in Phoenix or Scottsdale. Many buyers feel torn between the convenience of a spec home, the complete creative freedom of a custom build, and the balanced flexibility of a semi custom approach. Each option delivers unique benefits, costs and timelines, and the right fit depends on your lifestyle, budget and priorities.
What is a Spec Home?

A spec home (short for ‘speculative’) is a house that a builder constructs without a specific buyer in mind. Builders design these homes based on popular floor plans and finishes, then list them for sale once construction is underway or completed. The major appeal lies in speed and certainty. Because the plans and finishes are predetermined, you can walk through the model, know exactly what you’re getting and move in quickly. According to housing guides (Custom Home vs. Spec Home: Which Is Right for You?, Buying A Spec Home vs A Custom Build), spec homes are ideal for buyers who want a turnkey property with a predictable price point and minimal decisions to make. However, you sacrifice personalization and may need to budget for future upgrades if the standard selections don’t perfectly match your tastes.
What is a Custom Home?

A custom home starts with a blank sheet of paper. You choose the lot, the architect designs a one‑of‑a‑kind floor plan, and every detail, from the exterior elevation to the finishes, reflects your vision. Custom homes offer unparalleled personalization and can incorporate unique features such as RV garages, rooftop decks or a fully off‑grid solar design. As homebuilding sources note (Buying A Spec Home vs A Custom Build), custom builds require more time and typically cost more than spec homes because you are tailoring every element to your lifestyle. They also demand more involvement from the homeowner; you’ll work closely with your builder and designer to make hundreds of decisions along the way. The payoff is a residence that is uniquely yours and optimized for the desert climate.
What is a Semi Custom Home?

Semi custom homes bridge the gap between spec and fully custom. Builders offer a selection of pre‑designed floor plans and elevations, often popular layouts optimized for the local market – but allow buyers to make modifications to suit their needs. You might move walls, enlarge rooms, upgrade materials or add features like a casita or RV garage. Semi custom homes take less time than full custom builds because the core plan is already developed. They also offer cost savings over true custom construction, while still giving you the chance to personalize your home. With demand for semi custom homes growing across Southern Arizona (Accessory Dwelling Units (ADU) | City of Phoenix), this option offers a smart middle ground for families who want more than a spec home but don’t need a completely bespoke design.
Pros of Spec Homes in Arizona
Speed: With construction already underway or completed, you can move into a spec home in weeks rather than months or years.
Predictable Costs: The price is set, so you know exactly what your investment will be.
Low Stress: Minimal design decisions mean less time meeting with architects or agonizing over finishes.

Model Previews: You can tour completed homes to see the layout and craftsmanship before buying.Spec homes make sense for relocations, investment properties or buyers who prioritize convenience and are comfortable with standard features.
Pros of Custom Homes in Arizona
Total Personalization: Every aspect of the home – layout, materials, finishes – is tailored to you.
Site Optimization: Orientation and windows can be positioned to capture mountain views and manage solar gain.
Unique Features: Include specialized spaces such as RV garages, guest casitas, home offices or aging‑in‑place suites.

Long‑Term Value: A well‑designed custom home can appreciate more strongly because it stands out in the market.Custom homes are best for buyers who have a clear vision, a flexible timeline and the budget to bring their dream to life.
Pros of Semi Custom Homes in Arizona
Balanced Flexibility: Adjust a proven floor plan to your preferences without starting from scratch.
Faster Delivery: Because the base design is ready, construction moves faster than a fully custom home.
Cost Efficiency: Semi custom builds often come with transparent pricing and fewer surprises than custom projects.

Personal Touches: Add features such as energy‑efficient upgrades, smart‑home wiring or outdoor living spaces.Semi custom homes attract buyers who want personalization but also value a streamlined process.
How to Choose the Right Path
Budget: Determine how much flexibility you have for design and upgrades. A fixed price spec home may be appealing if you need to control costs.
Timeline: If you need to move quickly, spec or semi custom options can shave months off your move‑in date.
Lifestyle: Think about the features you can’t live without. For example, if you own a motorhome and want an integrated RV garage, spec homes rarely provide that, but semi custom or custom builds can.
Location: Evaluate communities and lots. Custom and semi custom homes allow you to design around specific views, while spec homes are limited to available inventory.

Future Plans: If you anticipate adding a guest house or ADU under Arizona’s new laws (Building ADUs and Guest Houses in Arizona: New Laws …, Accessory Dwelling Units (ADU) | City of Phoenix), it might be easier to incorporate that into a custom or semi custom plan.
Local Considerations for Phoenix & Scottsdale

The Sonoran Desert environment influences every homebuilding decision. Energy efficiency, sun management and outdoor living are major design drivers. When comparing spec, custom and semi custom options, consider features like passive solar orientation, high‑performance insulation, and shaded patios. Many Phoenix‑area homebuyers also look for RV garages, workshops and casitas to support an active lifestyle.
Final Thoughts

There’s no one‑size‑fits‑all answer. Spec homes deliver speed and convenience, custom homes provide complete freedom, and semi custom homes offer a blend of both. By weighing your priorities and working with an experienced builder like Jematell Homes, you can select the path that leads to your perfect Arizona home. Contact us today to discuss your goals and explore the best options for your dream build.

blocks.text: Spec Homes Explained: Benefits, Drawbacks and What to Expect in Phoenix and Scottsdale

Spec homes continue to gain popularity across Phoenix and Scottsdale as buyers look for faster timelines, modern designs, and pricing that feels more predictable than a full custom build. If you are exploring new construction in Arizona for 2026, understanding how spec homes work will help you decide whether this path fits your needs.
This guide breaks down what a spec home is, why many buyers choose one, the tradeoffs you should be aware of, and what the process looks like in the Valley.
What Is a Spec Home

A spec home is a new construction home built by a developer without a specific buyer in place. The builder selects the floor plan, finishes, layout, and materials based on what appeals to a wide range of buyers in the local market. Once construction is far enough along, the home becomes available for purchase.
Spec homes are common across high demand areas of the Phoenix metro including Scottsdale, North Phoenix, Desert Ridge, Gilbert, Mesa, and Casa Grande.
Key Benefits of Buying a Spec Home
Shorter Timeline

Spec homes are ideal for buyers who want new construction without waiting through a full design and build cycle. Many are available within weeks or a few months, compared to the longer timelines of a custom project.
Predictable Pricing
Builders set the price based on the completed or near completed product. This gives you clarity around total cost and reduces the chance of price increases that sometimes occur in custom builds.
Professional Design Choices

Spec homes are designed by professionals who understand Arizona trends and what buyers in Phoenix and Scottsdale prioritize. This can include energy efficient materials, popular floor plan layouts, desert friendly landscaping, and finishes that appeal to most buyers.
Move In Ready Convenience
Most spec homes are completely finished or close to finished. You can walk the home, see the layout in person, and know exactly what you are purchasing.
Energy Efficiency Features
New construction standards throughout Arizona continue to push for better insulation, high efficiency windows, and upgraded HVAC systems. Spec homes typically meet or exceed these standards.
Potential Drawbacks to Consider
Limited Customization

Because the home is already designed and built, you have fewer opportunities to personalize the layout or finishes. Some builders offer small upgrades if you buy early enough, but options are still limited.
Higher Competition
In popular areas like Scottsdale and North Phoenix, spec homes can sell quickly. Buyers who wait too long may lose out on the home they want.
Location Constraints
Spec homes are built on lots the builder already owns. If you want a specific neighborhood, lot size, orientation, or view, custom construction may give you more control.
What to Expect in the Phoenix and Scottsdale Markets
High Demand in 2026

The Phoenix metro continues to attract new residents, and builders are increasing spec home inventory to meet this demand. Expect competitive pricing on well designed floor plans and growing activity in areas south of Scottsdale, north of Phoenix, and expanding corridors such as Casa Grande.
Modern Desert Architecture
Most spec homes coming to the market feature open floor plans, large kitchens, covered patios, clean modern finishes, and neutral palettes that reflect current Arizona design preferences.
Energy Efficient Construction
Spray foam insulation, low emissivity windows, upgraded HVAC systems, and smart home features are becoming standard in spec homes throughout the region.
Straightforward Purchase Process
Buying a spec home is usually more streamlined than custom construction. You review the available home, sign your purchase agreement, and move in once construction reaches completion.
Is a Spec Home Right for You?

Choose a spec home if you want new construction without long waits, prefer predictable pricing, and are comfortable with finishes selected by professionals. It is a strong fit for buyers moving to Arizona, families upgrading their current home, and anyone who wants a modern living space without a full custom project.
Buyers who want complete design control or a specific lot location may be better suited for a custom or semi custom build.
A well built spec home in Phoenix or Scottsdale offers a balance of quality, convenience, and value, making it one of the most popular paths for 2026 home buyers in the Valley.
